transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Auto mute.
Sorry about that.
Doing well, how are you?
Yeah, I'm doing pretty good.
It's been a busy week, but I can see the weekend light at the end of the tunnel.
You feeling pretty good about your birds this year in Philly?
Oh, man.

That's a loaded question because I'm actually, you know, I'm here in Philly now, but I lived in New York for, um, 16 years and grew up in Jersey, so I'm unfortunately a Giants fan, and I don't feel good about them at all.
Hey, well, to maybe make you feel a little better, I'm in Houston here, and whether I was a Texans fan or a Cowboys fan, like I am, because my dad grew up in Dallas, either team is awful, so.
Yeah, yeah.
I mean, we had like a, the, the shootout of the, of the, the dregs of the division that last week, you know.
That was a wild.
Game, no doubt about it, but excited to chat with you and Rodolfo about GrowthX here today.

Just for a quick intro on my side, I'm the founding AEA here at GrowthX, and I work hand-in-hand with Marcel, our CEO, to really just explore, you know, how we could best help you, whether we're the right fit, and that's the plan for today, really, is just to get more context on your interests and give you a good understanding of what a partnership with GrowthX could look like.
Yeah, thank you.
Thank you, Tyler.
I just joined Doppel this week.
My mandate is basically digital marketing growth or growth on digital channels.
Joe here is managing our content, and so I definitely want to hear more about how GrowthX has evolved over time.
I actually know Jason and Marcel over LinkedIn.
There were a couple events that I missed, but I definitely saw Marcel in the last, like, 18 months or so, going into the team.
So for
On LinkedIn.
So I have a sense of like, hey, you guys are building like agentic workflows for SEO content from like keyword research.
How do we produce content, et cetera?
What I would love to do is like going deeper on like a couple of levels to like get joel up to speed on like, hey, what is the latest and greatest in terms of like workflows for SEO in particularly?
And then how do, how, how can we like get a taste of it to making sure we have a path going forward to grow the business?
That sounds great.
Rodolfo, how did you come across Marcel or Jason?
How did you hear about us?
I know Jason.
I know, I know Marcel on LinkedIn through like before when he was still in CMO sits like a few companies already.
So a long time.
Yeah.
Okay, cool.
Yeah.
I do feel like that's a big part of what makes us different is like the expertise and the first mover behind the service we're providing.
You know, Marcel like created all these workflows when he was a deep gram and had a lot of.
And that's what led to the origin of GrowthX.
I know you mentioned like, how are you different from Aerox?
And we'll talk more about that.
But not sure if you knew that we actually built our workflows in Aerox first before moving to our own internal platform.
So we actually, you know, have a history with Aerox.
We know the tool really well, some of the pros and cons.
And also, you know, a lot of the customers that they talk about, we've done like a lot of the content creation for.
So like Ramp is a great example there.
We've created almost a thousand pages for Ramp over the history.
Tell me more about, I guess, like, what's the role you're hoping content's going to play in like the overall marketing strategy?
Is that the biggest channel that you plan to invest in?
Do you plan to have content like alongside paid sort of 50-50?
What's your vision, I guess, at this stage?
I mean, I can answer from my perspective.
You know, I started at the company as a consultant just doing...
We content over a year ago, and that was like one of the primary channels we could do without very much investment.

You know, working with an SEO agency at the time that was providing strategy, and then I kind of took over that because we decided that we weren't getting the value from them, and I could do the same in terms of pulling strategy from SEMrush and assigning content from there.

Now I think it's way more of a balance, especially as Rodolfo's coming on, that we have dedicated folks for content, for advertising, you know, for each of these channels, and it should be more of a balance moving forward.
But, you know, previously we were a Series A startup until May and going with what resources we had, so it's building rapidly, but, you know, that sort of answers your question.
I mean, I see, you know, my background in building content teams at cybersecurity companies is, you know, I've been always a...
A proponent of using content as a growth channel in terms of making sure that every piece we publish is optimized for SEO, cetera, but also that we're doing a stream of content just for SEO.

And that's why we launched our Doppelpedia section on our website a few months ago because we wanted to separate out the sort of 101 SEO content with more subject matter and style leadership pieces on the blog, et cetera.
But that should just be the beginning, and I'm really sort of interested in hearing what you guys do in the content SEO work stream.
Yeah, absolutely.
And the content creators today, is it all in-house or do you partner with an agency for some of the content creation?
Well, we used to.
We parted ways with the agency and, you know, like it's part of our growth.
We're sort of switching.
partners around.
So we're between agencies at the moment, and I'm producing a lot of stuff with the assistance of ChatGPT and other tools.

You know, my goal in the very near future is to get freelance help again in order to produce a lot of the stuff that we were producing previously that sort of over the summer took a pause as we brought on, you know, our marketing team.
tripled over the last two months, basically.
So it's just been a lot at once, and so we're ready to sort of turn that back on again.
Super helpful kind of background context.
Let me jump in and just tell you a lot more about GrowthX, Joel, to help refine your understanding, and feel free to interrupt me as I go, ask any questions.
And I'll even move into, like, the customer examples, the AI workflows themselves, just try and give you, like, that complete picture.
then the for taking
So the big reason we're experiencing a lot of traction and having a lot of success with our clients is this new content marketing world, clicks are disappearing.
The first sales conversation is happening in ChatGPT or something similar.
And as a result, the AI search visits are showing a lot more intent, right?
4.4 times more likely to convert according to SEMrush, spend three times as long.
And so our approach, we split it into two different phases.
Phase one is a strategy sprint that's eight weeks.
Phase two is a 12-month growth execution where we'll do daily publishing.
And because our approach is audience first, combined with a lot of the traditional SEO approaches, we can benefit both sides, right?

So like Monograph is one of our customers showing up number one in traditional search for software for architects, also showing up as the number one option when someone goes in and gives more of that personal story.
Sorry.
Looking for a recommendation.
And so the reason that we've been able to have a lot of success with driving organic traffic, increasing LLM visibility, is because our workflows go through the most important factors for GEO.
So with research, we have our own deep research agent, and I'll show you what's happening with the workflow for the deep research.
In terms of attribution, we are only leveraging credible third-party sources for the research that we're doing.
Comprehensive, it speaks for itself.
We also do a lot of content refreshes and can even make the content really specific to your brand guidelines and your voice.

So our model has fit in really well where you could go out and buy a tool like Aerobs, but how long is it going to take you to get to a place where the workflows are delivering quality content that you can scale out?
Right?
What's going to happen when you're making all these different API calls?
So...
Bridging all these different tokens when it comes to costs.
You know, that was one reason that we built our own internal platform is it was just getting cost prohibitive on AirOps.

And then compared to the traditional agency, we're a partner who can do everything from SEO and GEO strategy all the way to staging and publishing in your CMS and providing analytics and visibility around LLMs so that you wouldn't need, you know, a tool like Profound, right?
And so that's been our approach is really just combining strategy, AI workflows to scale quality, experts in the loop to make sure that there's a good combination here.
And then that last mile execution.
And that's really growthx at a high level.
Any questions about that, Joel?
No, not at the moment.
What makes up the eight weeks research that you were mentioning?
Yeah, so the eight weeks, just to kind of jump ahead a little bit, what we're going to do is we're going to do our own.
Own independent research and SEO audit will ingest any resources that you can share.
So think maybe like a transcript from a good sales call, sales deck, brand documents, any documentation that you have around like your ICP or your positioning.

And so this combined with our kickoff call where we'll do, you know, specific discovery questions and dive deeper into the main competitors, your brand, all of that will actually start to inform these artifacts that we create by about week three, week four, and the eight-week engagement.
This provides the context engineering, yes, necessary to make our workflows super personalized and relevant.
But we also are going to publish during that eight-week strategy sprint.
So week five, week six, we'll be publishing by the point which you have to make a decision about sticking with phase two or opting out, which is the last week of phase one.
You'll have sample articles published.
You'll have a roadmap of the content that we're planning to create.
You'll understand the way that we're going to work with you so that you can make an educated decision about continuing to the daily publishing side of the partnership.
Got it.
It feels like the stage one is more just input than outcome driven, right?
What would you have to say about that?
I think, you know, phase one, it just sets us up for success with scaling.
If we're aligned on the inputs that will go into the workflows and the assignments that we're going to prioritize creating content around, then typically we can move super fast, right?
To your point, like we won't have a ton of performance data to look at in phase one, but you will understand how high quality our articles can get.
All the different assignments that we would work on in the next part of the partnership.
And if you wanted to walk away, you could keep the artifacts, use them for your own workflows.
So that's always a nice benefit.
Does that help address your question at all?
Yeah.
And then my second question then becomes on what types of SEO content you see most, or how does different types of SEO content fit into Phase 1 and 2?
Yeah, great question.
So a lot of times we start off with editorial as the foundation, just because it's so important for everything else that you might do.
In some situations, maybe we'll start off with a programmatic play, if you have something specific in mind, to give you some ideas of examples.
With town, they're in the accounting space.
We've done their entire blog, their seed stage, so really early on.
I like these examples because it shows how accurate we can get the content.
People talk about AI hallucinating.
We
We have a fact checker agent that we leverage.
And this allows town to feel really confident about every article basically being tax advice, right?
And all this different information that you can't get wrong.
It shows the way that like we can build in customizations to the content.
So they really wanted to have quotes from their own team and their own customers, which we were able to leverage in article generation.
And that's, you know, one example of a blog use case.
Another example of a blog use case in your space, Abnormal, is a great partner that we work with.
So we've done more like thought leadership, how AI is reshaping cybersecurity.
We've also done like more tactical pieces, eight metrics that prove the ROI of these types of investments.
And then to your point about Doppelpedia, we created this glossary for them, which is pretty similar here.
And there's like a page, right, for every part of the glossary.
So those are like the traditional.
Yeah, sorry.
Go ahead.
I was going to ask you, who in Abnormal do you work with?

Yeah, in Abnormal, I know that Abdallah is one of the main folks that we work with, but I joined about six weeks ago more on like the net new side, so I just have to like connect with the team to figure out our exact points of contact there.
Okay, okay.
Abdallah is one name I know for sure.
Okay.

My question is, you know, if you look under our blog, we've written a lot of similar kinds of pieces, and that's generally the recommendations we've gotten from both the SEO agencies we work with and are able to utilize, you know, a couple of different tools to produce these plus freelance writers.
Given the cost that you guys are sort of, it's a little bit more expensive, or I should say double the cost at least of our relationship with previous SEO agencies.
What's sort of the ROI from that?
And how- How
much better is the work that we're getting from GrowthX?
Yeah, super fair and great question.
I would say the first thing is the breadth of the service, right?
That agency partner, were they doing everything from strategy to staging and publishing in your CMS?
And were they doing anything around like GEO monitoring, right?
LLM analytics.
The breadth of our service, I would say, justifies the premium.
In addition to the fact that we're able to scale at a rate that we haven't seen other partners be able to match.
So being able to publish like a high quality, you know, article every single day of the week.
That would be another piece that you get out of our partnership.
I think when I show you the platform, Joel, and I show you the workflows, it'll sink in a bit more in terms of like what you're getting out of the GrowthX partnership.
But that's high level what I would say.
Yeah, it's interesting because, you know, I've created it.
Similar program in the past with using an outside group of freelance writers and where it gets expensive in that sense is, you know, it's not just the strategy, but also the execution of posting.

Like you said, we were at, you know, at a previous company, for example, I was paying $2,000 an article, which gets really expensive if you're posting four or five times a week, you know, and, you know, theoretically up to 20 articles a month, it gets very expensive.
So then that makes your model more cost effective if you're sort of looking to post that much and it's all included.
Exactly.
It's a good way to think about it.

I mean, I've heard Marcel say, you know, if you wanted to do this in-house, right, you would need like a head of content or a managing editor, you would, you know, need an AI workflow platform now, right, or some sort of GTM engineer.

You'd need writers and editors, as you mentioned, you'd need an SEO agency, you'd need graphic design freelancers, and that's a good way to think about the cost, right, in-house versus the cost of growth acts.
To your point, you know, I think Augment's a good example of like a case study.
The Augment and the AI coding space, they wanted to compare the guides that we built, so the blog that they had built in-house.

And so we built out this new section of their site where we're doing everything here in terms of the images that you see, in terms of when there's tables in the content or like different charts that we're comparing tools.
And these guides, it's all, you know, the AI coding space, recommendations, one tool versus another.
It's really like a thought leadership play for them.
And we've seen that their guides have had 2x the traffic of their blog.
And last week alone delivered like 300 new signups for them.
We're also seeing that a lot of these guides that we've written for some of like the main keywords that they're targeting.
And, you know, this stuff like changes all the time, right?
But Augment is now showing up in the answer where...
They were seeing, you know, Sourcegraph, they were seeing GitHub, they were seeing Cursor.
And now, you know, you can see that we've got one article at the very top, another article there, citation directly to the Augment site.
And this is just kind of like a good look at the growth that they've seen since becoming a partner, which is partly their space, right?
Not all us, but partly like the high-quality approach that we can scale out as well.
That makes a lot of sense.

I will say, you know, it should be standards, not that it's, I love that you guys are talking about GEO, but in my opinion, it's standard practice for, if an SEO agency isn't talking about GEO, they're like, you know, ancient history.

So we definitely have gotten that, I meant to say that before, I've gotten that service from an SEO agency, but not, you know, the comprehensive start-to-finish publication, which is like, that's honestly the most attractive.

Part of the service to me, because instinctively, if you've worked in SEO and been with the company a while, know cybersecurity, and we know our core terms, we're going after sort of the specific subject matter on a regular basis and monitoring that ourselves in SEMrush.
What we don't have is an efficient way to produce content on a daily basis, and I think that's where this becomes really attractive to me.
Yeah, absolutely.
That's a good way to think about it.
How technical does the content need to be?
It's not to say we can't do it.
It's just more so if every, you know, article you wanted to post was something that a cybersecurity engineer would need to, like, weigh in on, then that's where it could get a little tricky.
No, and I think that's what's like what my goal is to try and have a couple of different streams of content.
You know, this would be one stream that like, and always on content for SEO and organic traffic channel.
And then separately have.

Those thought leadership pieces that I'm working on publishing with our tech teams that have a lot of technical information, like these, if you look on our blog, there's a couple of threat intelligence briefs, you know, so like those, I can't do without their help, and I wouldn't want to leave to an LLM because there's too many, you know, like a lot of times, we have to redact a lot of information and take customer names out and different things that we know.
We need sort of human sensitivities for.
Those are the kind of pieces that are heavier lifts and take a team to do.

But, you know, the, you know, listicles, I think we would probably still be running listicles on the blog, but then also separately, the Doppelpedia pieces are the ones that we could theoretically hand off to a growthx and have that run on its own, you know, in tandem with our heavier lift pieces of
Content that take more team members.
Definitely.
I think, you know, these are good examples of programmatic plays that we've done for Ramp.
So we built this expense for DM Calculator, and there's this interactive map, but there's also pages for every single city.
So anytime someone's looking up for DM in a city, you know, Ramp wants to be connected to the answer.
Same thing for their vendor directory.
We use their internal data to populate all these different charts around how much businesses spend on certain tools.
There's alternatives as well as comparisons in this vendor directory.
And then we also built this expense classifier for them.
And so these are things that we can think about, like, after we've built a good foundation in your blog.
Absolutely.
And that's another sort of work stream altogether that this could support theoretically in terms of more, you know, down funnel product related.
Consideration stage content that right now we don't have a lot of bandwidth to produce.
Until recently, we're a very small team.
So, I mean, that's tremendously valuable, I think, too.
And I like this sort of hub-and-spoke model with that kind of calculator that you guys created.
Is all of that created with the tool, or is there some sort of editorial team that supports all that?
Because, yeah, I'm just curious, like, how it's going to be.
Yeah, so, like, we will use the platform, which I'll show you next, for our internal team only right now.
Although we're bringing clients in very soon.
And I'll show you what the clients would have access to.
The platform's primarily used to draft the content, right?
And then we would have you review the content.
To actually stage and publish, we'd need credentials to access your CMS.
And that's what we're doing is just logging into the client's CMS and doing the last-mile execution from there.
Do you know if you guys are SOC2 compliant?
So we're planning to get SOC 2 done in H1 next year, but today we're also like working with Sentinel 1, Okta, so I'm sure we could find some way to work together.
Yeah, I'm just curious because I know, you know, we are, but I think we've worked with other vendors that don't necessarily have a certification.
I was just curious.
Yeah, the big thing is just like not having PII and not sending resources to us that have sensitive data.
Those are the workarounds that usually help since there's not PII that we're leveraging as part of the relationship.
Right.
Interestingly, know, we're also, you know, one of our other new teammates who is someone on Rodolfo's work before us, a gentleman named Hamza, who is rebuilding our website as we speak.
So like all of this stuff is currently like in major construction, but, you know, it would be interesting to see once we get that up and running, what we might be able to do.
Are you guys changing CMSs or just doing some?
Redesign work?
All of the above.
Which CMS are you moving to and when do you think you'll be on the new CMS?
Drupal and it sounds like in the next month.
Okay.
Okay, perfect.
Yeah, I'll go through a quick demo of how a piece of content gets created.
And then I'd love to bring Marcel maybe to the next call where we can do a deeper dive.
And, you know, any questions I haven't answered well enough for you today, Marcel can weigh in much, much more granularly.
So in this workspace here, Engine, they're in the business travel space.
We've done all these different business travel guides for them.
And what's cool about this workspace is you see how we're flexible around the artifacts.
So we built artifacts for every target industry they had.
And if you look at one of these artifacts here in terms of like our audience first approach, you'll be able to see.
see.
If I can load the page.
Oh, I guess I can't.
Give it one more try, and then I'll just talk about it.
There we go.
Okay, perfect.
So it's, you know, who is this person?
What are their fears, uncertainties, motivations?
How do you win and lose their trust?
What will get them promoted?
What will get them fired?
Right?
We're basically trying to get in the mind of your ICP.

Right?
So all of our workflows are built in code.
Makes it very easy to create custom workflows.
Easy to refactor with new models.
Easy to improve our workflows over time.

But the first step, we're taking keyword, direction from our managing editor, and then artifacts.
And all of that is creating this robust brief.
Now, if I inspect this workflow, you'll see that what's happening for that first step is a lot of things in parallel in the background.
So we're going in and we're looking at keyword traffic, organic results, we're looking at SERP ranking factors and competitor content.
This is, you know, something that you guys don't have to pay for any of the tools and API calls and tokens that we're leveraging.
You know, this is going to connect to SEMrush, Ahrefs.
We use Tavli and Exa for deep research.
We're also leveraging multiple different models at any given time.
And all of this informs that brief.

But as you continue through the process, you'll see that, you know, our next step is to go in, generate research questions, perform deep research against each question, and then validate that against credible sources.
And so it's building upon itself in each step of the process, while the managing editor has the ability to make changes directly to the content, or even use.
AI with the context from the artifacts to suggest changes and auto-generate them.
I'm sorry, the managing editor, is that someone, is that us or you?
Yeah, good question.
So you have on the phase two, phase one, it's like a dedicated sprint team that works with all of our new customers.
And then phase two, you have a managing director, a managing editor, and a content manager from GrowthX that's assigned to your account.
So that consists of folks who are, you know, SEO experts and former, like, journalists, writers, in addition to, like, content marketers.
And these are just, like, some of the places where those folks have worked.
One thing, I guess, is, yeah, that's right.
That's like you're describing me to a T, honestly.
It's kind of funny.
Yeah.
So, I mean, the goal is to be an extension of your team, essentially, right?
So, like, if you guys are doing content...
It can be more around your pillar pieces.
It could be very expert-generated content through some of your technical folks.
And then you can let us handle the heavy lifting on the stuff that is just hard to do without a lot of time and effort.
Yeah.
Yeah, I know for the interest of time, do you have separate videos going in details on the platform, or it has to be another sales call?
I think we should probably, we don't have a video on it, but we could set up the call with Marcel so that we could get really tactical around the workflows.
Would that be of interest for you both?
Let's set it up first.
I'm just trying to navigate on how do we move forward.
What I also want to move quickly, like how, like the only inhibitor or major inhibitor to me is cost.
Probably we have.
Field Series B, we are early stage, everything is like building while flying, right?
What do you have to say in that regard?
Right now, we're not, you know, strained for like demand.
Essentially, we can choose the clients that we're working with each week.
And so 15K, it is like a hard floor for any new client that we'll work with.
But I do think that if you had, you know, maybe one conversation with Marcel next week, you could get some better insight around comparing to Aerox or comparing to another agency.
But yeah, I mean, we have some seed in Series A that pay us that price.
And, you know, some even pay up front, others pay semi-annual.
So we can be flexible on payment terms, but the 15K would be solid.
Okay.
Um, but, you know, um, and then, and then it's just like as simple.
But it's like go, no, go, and then end of phase one, is there like a lead time?
You'd have until the last week of phase one.
So seven out of those eight weeks, you could opt out of the phase two partnership.
And we can naturally like have more conversations to make you feel more confident about what you'd be getting out of growthx before we start phase one.
Essentially, we're talking about like, let's see, you know, upwards of $220,000.
investment altogether.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Two, yeah, two, two, two, 10, you guys would be at 30, not 40, but yeah, you're spot on.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Okay.
Okay, let's have the call.
Anyway, I think, I think to me, it's just around the budget conversation and what we can do there.
And then, um, whether we even have all the material.
Yeah.
Those that you guys need, is that another question?
Like, if we don't have all you need, like, there's also no point to stop, right?
Because I want to make sure, like, I would equip with the best information that you guys have.
If you were too early for it, that's okay, too.
So I appreciate all that.
If you can send me the recording as well as the slides that you can share, that would be really helpful.
And then Joe and I can take that internally, and then we can schedule a next call with Marcel as well.
That's perfect.
And if we could just maybe just nail down a placeholder, since his calendar's crazy, I've got Thursday the 2nd from 10 to 11 a.m.
Pacific, or 2 to 3 p.m.
Pacific, if there's a time for 30 minutes in either hour.
Thursday, let me check Joe's calendar.
Thursday at 10 should be fine.
Thursday at 10 Pacific.
Perfect.
Great.
I'll hold that, and that'll give you a good chance to ask any of those lingering questions that...
Can help you make the right decision.
And also I think Marcel can give you good insight on whether, you know, you're too early or at the right timing.
I think definitely make sure you're on the new CMS before we start working together.
But other than that, you know, we have worked with Seed and Series A, so it wouldn't be abnormal or anything like that.
Yeah, I think we've had one Thursday, October 2nd.
Correct.
I do, I actually have a conflict at 1 p.m.
Eastern, but I'm free at 1.30 or, or any time later than that.
Could you do 1.30 or 2 p.m.
Pacific?
1.30 Eastern, so I'm in Philly, as you mentioned before, but just so we...
10.30 Pacific, 1.30 Eastern.
Yes, Yes, it.
Thank you.
But, Rodolfo, you were saying something here in closing?
No.
Ah, I forgot the thought.
Sorry.

Perfect.
I'll share some more resources that you can review between now and then.
But we'll talk next week and thanks for reaching out.
Yeah, I am.

Yeah, definitely.
Hit Marcel with all the hard questions.
He likes to have that conversation.
I'll see you all next week.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Introduce GrowthX's AI-powered content marketing services to Doppel and explore potential partnership.](https://fathom.video/share/dzsy6z5_Ce8WDPWob6DcJZrs3K9DTJvt?tab=summary&timestamp=1.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX offers comprehensive content marketing services, including SEO/GEO strategy, AI-powered content creation, and CMS publishing](https://fathom.video/share/dzsy6z5_Ce8WDPWob6DcJZrs3K9DTJvt?tab=summary&timestamp=65.0)
  - [Doppel is interested but concerned about cost ($15K/month) given their Series B stage](https://fathom.video/share/dzsy6z5_Ce8WDPWob6DcJZrs3K9DTJvt?tab=summary&timestamp=1730.0)
  - [A follow-up call with Marcel (GrowthX CEO) is scheduled to address lingering questions and determine fit](https://fathom.video/share/dzsy6z5_Ce8WDPWob6DcJZrs3K9DTJvt?tab=summary&timestamp=1889.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX Service Overview

  - [Two-phase approach: 8-week strategy sprint, followed by 12-month growth execution](https://fathom.video/share/dzsy6z5_Ce8WDPWob6DcJZrs3K9DTJvt?tab=summary&timestamp=451.0)
  - [Combines AI workflows with human expertise for scalable, high-quality content](https://fathom.video/share/dzsy6z5_Ce8WDPWob6DcJZrs3K9DTJvt?tab=summary&timestamp=566.0)
  - [Handles everything from strategy to CMS publishing and LLM analytics](https://fathom.video/share/dzsy6z5_Ce8WDPWob6DcJZrs3K9DTJvt?tab=summary&timestamp=550.0)
  - [Can produce daily high-quality articles, outpacing traditional agencies](https://fathom.video/share/dzsy6z5_Ce8WDPWob6DcJZrs3K9DTJvt?tab=summary&timestamp=936.0)

### Doppel's Current Content Approach

### GrowthX Differentiators

  - [Proprietary AI platform with customizable workflows](https://fathom.video/share/dzsy6z5_Ce8WDPWob6DcJZrs3K9DTJvt?tab=summary&timestamp=1532.0)

### Technical Considerations
